questions.prompt: Why use the pooled connection (port 6543) instead of direct (5432) for app traffic?

choices.text: Postgres connections are expensive — each eats ~10MB and a backend process. A Node.js or serverless app that spawns new connections per request will exhaust the database's connection cap quickly. Port 6543 is PgBouncer in transaction-mode: connections are leased per transaction and returned to a small backend pool, letting hundreds of app clients share a small number of real DB connections. Direct connection (5432) bypasses the pooler — use for migrations and tools that need session-level features, NEVER for app traffic at scale.

questions.explanation: Pooled (6543) for app; direct (5432) only for migrations and session-dependent tools.

questions.prompt: Common query suddenly slow after adding RLS. Diagnostic and fix?

choices.text: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the query (in a session impersonating the user) to see the plan. Common findings: (1) sequential scan on policy-referenced columns — add an index, (2) helper functions in the policy being called per row instead of per query — mark them `STABLE`, (3) EXISTS subqueries on join tables (memberships, shares) with no index — add a composite index like memberships(user_id, team_id). Most RLS performance issues are fixable through indexes and function attributes; don't disable RLS — fix the indexes.

questions.explanation: EXPLAIN → index policy columns → STABLE helpers → index EXISTS join tables.
